title: Japanese accordion album with hard covers
subtitle: A leporello-fold book with board covers for photographs and prints
excerpt: A Japanese accordion album, sometimes called a leporello, is a single long sheet folded back and forth in a concertina, with hard boards glued to the first and last panels as covers. It opens in a continuous panorama or folds flat. A simple structure with no sewing, completed in one session.
sourceNotes: The accordion or leporello fold is documented in nineteenth-century bookbinding manuals including Cockerell's Bookbinding and the Care of Books (1901). The Japanese variant uses a single sheet of washi rather than joined papers; the covered version attaches boards as described in Cockerell.

glossaryTerms.definition: A book structure made from a single sheet folded concertina-style into equal panels; named after Don Giovanni's servant in Mozart's opera who unfolds a long list of conquests.
A series of parallel valley and mountain folds creating an equal panel concertina, so the structure can expand into a panorama or compress flat.

attrs.title: Cutting tools
attrs.body: Use sharp blades only; change when they dull. Cut on a self-healing mat against a metal ruler, non-cutting hand well back.

content.text: The
leporello

needs no sewing and no complex adhesive work. It produces a panoramic photograph album or a display fold in under an hour. The version described here makes an A5 album with eight panels plus two board covers.
What you need

items.name: Cartridge paper, 220 gsm, cut to 21 cm x 160 cm (A5 height by eight A5 panels)
items.substitutions: Two strips of 80 cm each, joined with a 10 mm overlapping butt joint; washi kozo is the traditional Japanese alternative

items.name: Bookbinding board, 2 mm, cut to A5 plus 3 mm on three sides
Book cloth or decorative paper for covers
items.qty: enough to cover both boards with 20 mm turn-in

content.text: Folding the text block
Cut the long strip to exactly 160 cm (eight 20 cm panels for an A5 album). Check the grain direction: fold parallel to the grain.
Fold the strip in half lengthways, then in half again, then in half once more. The result is eight equal panels.
Open the strip completely. Refold it
accordion-style
by alternating valley and mountain folds at each crease. Score each fold with the bone folder against a ruler for a crisp edge.
Press the folded block flat under weight for 30 minutes.
Making the covers
Cut the covering material 20 mm larger than each board on all four sides. Paste with PVA, lay the board, fold the turn-ins and mitre the corners.
Cut a liner paper for the inside of each cover and paste it down.
Leave covers to dry under weight for 1 hour.
Attaching covers to the text block
Brush PVA onto the liner of the front cover. Press the first panel of the folded text block firmly onto it, aligning the edges. Rub down with the bone folder.
Repeat for the back cover and the last panel.
Press the closed album under a flat board and weights for 2 hours before use.
Finishing

The album opens as a panorama (all panels in sequence) or as a conventional page-turn book from the front cover. It can be displayed accordion-open on a shelf. If ribbon ties are wanted, glue them to the inside of the covers before attaching the text block, as described in the portfolio tutorial.
